## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

6597303f  ₢BBAAX    First-run inline fix for pristine-lifecycle/rbtdrp_depot_tear_down: zrbgp_depot_state_emit's classification path was reading state directly from CRM v3 projects:search hits, which Google documents as eventually consistent and lags mutations — notably DELETE_REQUESTED transitions post-projects.delete. After a successful rbgp_depot_unmake (which polls projects.get up to 60s waiting for DELETE_REQUESTED at the strongly-consistent endpoint), a subsequent rbgp_depot_list call observed the unmade project still as state=ACTIVE in search and emitted COMPLETE in the fact file, failing the fixture's verify. Reshape: search remains for discovery (display-name-anchored project enumeration), but per-hit state classification now issues a one-shot GET against ${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects/${projectId} — the same URL pattern rbgp_depot_unmake itself uses for verify-deletion at line 1061. Reordered loop body so projectId/displayName/moniker validation runs before the GET (skip the HTTP round trip on non-domain projects). Three new per-iteration locals (z_get_url, z_get_infix, z_get_code) declared in the BCG exception-2 block. 200 -> classify on returned .state; 404 -> skip iteration silently (project completed deletion between search and GET, fixture's absent-fact-file path treats as success); other -> log and skip, mirroring existing unrecognized-state handling. No new helper minted: surveyed rbgu_poll_until_ok (wait-for-availability), rbgu_http_json_ok (die-on-non-2xx), rbgu_http_require_ok-with-warn-code (rbgg_Governor.sh:746-750 precedent for 4xx tolerance) — none was the right shape for one-shot-read-with-skip-on-404, and only one current call site needs this composition. Inlined three primitives (rbgu_http_json + rbgu_http_code_capture + rbgu_json_field_capture) at the use site, modeled on the codebase's established posture of inlining tolerant 4xx handling rather than abstracting it. shellcheck -S error clean. Cost: 1 search call + N GET calls per list invocation (was 1 search call); for typical 4-10 depots this adds 4-10 HTTP round trips — acceptable for dev/test scale infrastructure.

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

After a successful rbgp_depot_unmake (which polls projects.get up to 60s waiting for DELETE_REQUESTED at the strongly-consistent endpoint), a subsequent rbgp_depot_list call observed the unmade project still as state=ACTIVE in search and emitted COMPLETE in the fact file, failing the fixture's verify. Reshape: search remains for discovery (display-name-anchored project enumeration), but per-hit state classification now issues a one-shot GET against ${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects/${projectId} — the same URL pattern rbgp_depot_unmake itself uses for verify-deletion at line 1061. Reordered loop body so projectId/displayName/moniker validation runs before the GET (skip the HTTP round trip on non-domain projects). Three new per-iteration locals (z_get_url, z_get_infix, z_get_code) declared in the BCG exception-2 block. 200 -> classify on returned .state; 404 -> skip iteration silently (project completed deletion between search and GET, fixture's absent-fact-file path treats as success); other -> log and skip, mirroring existing unrecognized-state handling. No new helper minted: surveyed rbgu_poll_until_ok (wait-for-availability), rbgu_http_json_ok (die-on-non-2xx), rbgu_http_require_ok-with-warn-code (rbgg_Governor.sh:746-750 precedent for 4xx tolerance) — none was the right shape for one-shot-read-with-skip-on-404, and only one current call site needs this composition. Inlined three primitives (rbgu_http_json + rbgu_http_code_capture + rbgu_json_field_capture) at the use site, modeled on the codebase's established posture of inlining tolerant 4xx handling rather than abstracting it. shellcheck -S error clean. Cost: 1 search call + N GET calls per list invocation (was 1 search call); for typical 4-10 depots this adds 4-10 HTTP round trips — acceptable for dev/test scale infrastructure.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAX
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
158	
159	**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
160	
161	Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
162	
163	## Cost
164

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAX

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; BBAAX exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

**Paddock summary.** ₣BB constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third release tier that **refuses to run unless marshal-zero was just committed**, designed to catch silent first-build assumptions that `rbw-tf`/`rbw-tr` tolerate. It exec's the gauntlet suite (11 fixtures: enrollment-validation → pristine-lifecycle → canonical-establish → canonical-onboarding-sequence → regime-validation → regime-smoke → four-mode → tadmor → moriah → srjcl → pluml). dd5893ba jjb:1011-f418a7e7:₢BBAAX:n: Add Mid-execution posture rule to JJK agent context, sourced from rules-of-engagement conversation during BBAAX gauntlet first-run. Captures recurring failure mode where mid-pace surprises trigger option-proliferation responses (multi-option triage menus, scope/cost weighing, slate-vs-inline frame, follow-up-pace recommendations) that consume operator context with segmentation work that's not the agent's territory. Rule prescribes mechanism + one specific repair as default response shape; explicit prohibition on proliferating options or weighing consequences across alternatives; reaffirms that pace scope and segmentation are operator territory. Placed after Paddock posture in the agent-behavior framing cluster (Sincerity / Docket posture / Slate-time / Stale-by-mount / Docket anti-patterns / Reference discipline / Paddock posture), before Identities vs Display Names where the structural vocabulary begins. Located in jjk-claude-context.md rather than top-level CLAUDE.md because the failure mode is JJK-vocabulary-induced — outside a mount the pace-language doesn't load and the segmentation temptation doesn't surface; killing the temptation at the JJK context level is cleaner than blacklisting symptoms in a general directive.

content.content:
48de4cae Marshal Zero — release qualification reset
ddb9fd9f jjb:1011-d86518ca:₢A-AAo:n: Add 'Enable Password Authentication in sshd_config' step to Windows handbook procedure (between Start/Enable Service and Allow Firewall Port). Empirical finding from rocket bootstrap: Windows OpenSSH 9.5 ships with sshd_config in a state that blocks password auth — the wire protocol advertises keyboard-interactive (the standard wrapper for password delivery on Windows) but the server refuses to actually prompt, producing 'Permission denied (publickey,keyboard-interactive)' with no prompt ever shown to the operator. Microsoft Learn confirms: on Windows OpenSSH the only configurable AuthenticationMethods are 'password' and 'publickey' (KbdInteractiveAuthentication is in the not-available list); the server's PasswordAuthentication directive controls both wire methods. Default-shipped state empirically rejects password auth even when the wire protocol advertises it. New step uses notepad on BUBC_windows_sshd_config (operator already has elevated PowerShell so SYSTEM-owned write succeeds), instructs operator to set PasswordAuthentication yes (or add the line if absent), then Restart-Service sshd. Step prose explains this is temporary state — garrison flips back to 'no' as part of its hardening. Placement is post-Start/Enable (sshd_config doesn't exist until first sshd start) and pre-Firewall (so the server is in the correct state when first reachable from network). Step 3 (Start/Enable) gains a one-line note that first start generates the default sshd_config. Reused BUBC_windows_sshd_config (had been retired from handbook in earlier trim, now re-adopted for this step — single source of truth preserved). Validated via tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh: Windows section now reads 5 numbered steps (Set Password / Install / Start+Enable / Enable PasswordAuth / Firewall) followed by Verify Reachability and Run Garrison sections.
e170740c jjb:1011-2615224d:₢A-AAo:n: Add explicit 'set or confirm admin password' step to handbook — don't presume operator already knows the local admin password (Windows logon is often via PIN/Hello/Microsoft-account; Linux admin user may have no password set). Windows: new Step 1 (Set or Confirm Admin Password) using `net user <admin-user> <temp-password>`, with note that the value stops affecting SSH after garrison hardens sshd_config; existing 3 steps renumber to 2/3/4 via auto-counter. Dropped redundant 'Admin user account has a known password' precondition bullet (now covered by step). Linux/Mac: parallel guidance added — `sudo passwd <admin-user>` if unsure of the password, with same 'value stops affecting SSH after garrison runs' framing. Operators who prefer pre-establishing key trust can still use ssh-copy-id (kept as alternative). Verified via tt/buw-hj0.HandbookJurisdictionTop.sh: Windows section now 4 numbered steps + Verify Reachability + Run Garrison; Linux/Mac section reads cleanly as two paragraphs (manual scope + password setup) plus the optional ssh-copy-id alternative. Landing prose unchanged — 'make sshd reachable on the network with a known admin password' is now backed by explicit guidance for ensuring that password is known.

content.text:
**Mechanism.** GCP grants implicit Owner to the principal that calls `projects.create`. The depot `canc-canest2100004` was just created by your **Payor OAuth refresh token**, which is bound to one specific Google account (the one that authorized `rbw-gPI PayorInstall`). The browser console is signed into a *different* Google account — it has no IAM grants on this fresh project, hence missing `cloudbuild.builds.get`/`list`.

Recipe Bottle stores only `RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET` + `RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN` in `../station-files/secrets/payor/rbro.env`, not the email — so I can't tell you the exact identity from here. But it's the account that consented when you ran `rbw-gPE PayorEstablish` (Manor establishment) → `rbw-gPI PayorInstall` (OAuth grant).

**Specific repair.** Click the profile chip top-right in the console and switch to the Payor's Google account. That session will have full Owner on the depot project via the implicit creation grant.

Cross-context tell: your tab strip shows Tailscale-on-Windows browsing — if this is a Windows session and the Payor OAuth was authorized on macOS, the browser default account is almost certainly the wrong one.

message.content: What is a 'profile chip'?

content.text:
The small circular avatar/initial in the top-right corner of the Google Cloud console — in your screenshot it's the rightmost element on the dark header bar, just past the bell/notification icon. Click it; a dropdown appears showing the current signed-in account email, a "Sign out" option, and (if you're signed into multiple Google accounts) "Switch account" / a list of other available accounts.

That dropdown both **tells you which account you're using** and **lets you switch** to the Payor's account.

content.text:
Diagnosed — **my project-ID construction was wrong**. `rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:84` injects a `d-` infix between cloud prefix and depot moniker:

```
RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}d-${RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER}"
                     = canc- + d- + canest2100004
                     = canc-d-canest2100004
```

The URL I gave you (`canc-canest2100004`) points to a different project — either nonexistent or someone else's, hence the access denial. Correct URL:

```
https://console.cloud.google.com/cloud-build/builds?project=canc-d-canest2100004
```

Sorry for the misdirect.

message.content: Ah that was the fix.  Is there something in the codebase to fix to make this more correct in the future?

content.text:
The most direct lever: when `rbgp_Payor.sh` / `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` actually fires a Cloud Build trigger, it already has `RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID` in scope — emit the console URL on that line so the gauntlet output stream carries `https://console.cloud.google.com/cloud-build/builds?project=canc-d-canest2100004` directly, no reconstruction needed.

Tradeoff: one extra `buc_info` per build, plus a thin assumption that Google's console URL shape (`project=X` query param) stays stable — which it has been for years.

Want me to slate this as a follow-up pace under ₣BB (or ₣BA cleanups, since it's not a gauntlet correctness issue)? Or inline it now?

message.content: just make a fix now and notch.

19:# Recipe Bottle Foundry Director Build - ordain, conjure, enshrine, kludge, mirror, graft operations (director credentials)
47:  # Acronym: rbgjb = Recipe Bottle Google Json Build (step scripts in rbgjb/ dir)
211:  local -r z_metric_encoded="cloudbuild.googleapis.com%2Fconcurrent_private_pool_build_cpus"
212:  local -r z_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_SERVICEUSAGE}${RBGC_SERVICEUSAGE_V1BETA1}/projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/services/cloudbuild.googleapis.com/consumerQuotaMetrics/${z_metric_encoded}"
254:    buc_warn "Build quota insufficient: ${z_limit} vCPU quota / ${z_vcpus} vCPUs per build = ${z_max_concurrent} concurrent (need ${RBRR_GCB_MIN_CONCURRENT_BUILDS})"
263:# to Cloud Build. Checks two layers in dependency order:
271:# A missing image at either layer causes a Cloud Build failure minutes later.
465:  # Build strategy: compare vessel platforms against runner platform
476:    buc_log_args "Build strategy: ${z_build_strategy} — rbgjb02 included"
479:    buc_log_args "Build strategy: ${z_build_strategy} — rbgjb02 excluded"
580:  # _RBGA_BUILD_ID is the lone exception — Cloud Build job ID, only available
594:  # Build ID: $BUILD_ID → GCB built-in available as env var
628:  # Compose builds.create Build resource

rbgu_http_json "POST" "${ZRBFC_GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL}" "${z_token}" \
    "enshrine_build_create" "${z_build_file}"
  rbgu_http_require_ok "Enshrine build submission" "enshrine_build_create"

  local z_build_id=""
  z_build_id=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "enshrine_build_create" '.metadata.build.id') || z_build_id=""
  test -n "${z_build_id}" || buc_die "Build ID not found in builds.create response"

  local -r z_console_url="${ZRBFC_CLOUD_QUERY_BASE};region=${RBGD_GCB_REGION}/${z_build_id}?project=${RBGD_GCB_PROJECT_ID}"
  buc_info "Enshrine build submitted: ${z_build_id}"
  buc_link "Click to " "Open build in Cloud Console" "${z_console_url}"

  zrbfc_wait_build_completion 50 "Enshrine"  # ~4 minutes at 5s intervals
}

# Extract build ID from Operation response
  local z_build_id=""
  z_build_id=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "build_direct_create" '.metadata.build.id') || z_build_id=""
  test -n "${z_build_id}" || buc_die "Build ID not found in builds.create response"
  echo "${z_build_id}" > "${ZRBFC_BUILD_ID_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to persist build ID"

  local -r z_console_url="${ZRBFC_CLOUD_QUERY_BASE};region=${RBGD_GCB_REGION}/${z_build_id}?project=${RBGD_GCB_PROJECT_ID}"
  buc_info "Build dispatched: ${z_build_id}"
  buc_link "Click to " "Open build in Cloud Console" "${z_console_url}"

  zrbfc_wait_build_completion 960 "Conjure"  # 80 minutes at 5s intervals

  # Consistency assert: verify Cloud Build echoed back the same hallmark we minted
  buc_step "Verifying hallmark consistency"

      projectId: $projectId,
      displayName: $displayName
    }' > "${z_create_project_body}" || buc_die "Failed to build project creation body"

  local -r z_create_project_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects"
  rbgu_http_json_lro_ok \
    "Create depot project" \
    "${z_token}" \
    "${z_create_project_url}" \
    "depot_project_create" \
    "${z_create_project_body}" \
    ".name" \
    "${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V1}" \
    "operations/" \
    "${RBGC_EVENTUAL_CONSISTENCY_SEC}" \
    "${RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC}"

  buc_step 'Link billing account'
  local -r z_billing_body="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_billing_link.json"
  jq -n \
    --arg billingAccountName "billingAccounts/${RBRP_BILLING_ACCOUNT_ID}" \
    '{
      billingAccountName: $billingAccountName
    }' > "${z_billing_body}" || buc_die "Failed to build billing link body"

  local -r z_billing_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CLOUDBILLING}${RBGC_CLOUDBILLING_V1}/projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/billingInfo"
  rbgu_http_json "PUT" "${z_billing_url}" "${z_token}" "depot_billing_link" "${z_billing_body}"
  rbgu_http_require_ok "Link billing account" "depot_billing_link"

  buc_step 'Get depot project number'
  local -r z_project_info_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V3}/projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}"
  rbgu_http_json "GET" "${z_project_info_url}" "${z_token}" "depot_project_info"
  rbgu_http_require_ok "Get project info" "depot_project_info"

  local z_project_number
  # CRM v3 returns project number in name field as "projects/{number}"
  z_project_number=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "depot_project_info" '.name | sub("projects/"; "")') || buc_die "Failed to get project number"
  test -n "${z_project_number}" || buc_die "Project number is empty"

  buc_step 'Enable depot project APIs'
  local -r z_api_services="artifactregistry cloudbuild cloudresourcemanager containeranalysis iam serviceusage storage"
  for z_service in ${z_api_services}; do
    rbgu_api_enable "${z_service}" "${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}" "${z_token}"
  done

  buc_step 'Create dual worker pools (tether + airgap)'
  local -r z_pool_parent="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CLOUDBUILD}${RBGC_CLOUDBUILD_V1}/projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${z_region}${RBGC_PATH_WORKER_POOLS}"

  # Tether pool (default egress — public internet access)
  local -r z_tether_id="${RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM}${RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_TETHER}"
  local -r z_tether_create_url="${z_pool_parent}?workerPoolId=${z_tether_id}"
  local -r z_tether_create_body="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_pool_tether_create.json"

file.content:
rbgp_depot_levy() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  local -r z_region="${RBRR_GCP_REGION}"

  buc_doc_brief "Create new depot infrastructure following RBAGS specification"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step 'Authenticate as Payor'
  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) || buc_die "Failed to authenticate as Payor via OAuth"

  buc_step 'Validate region against Artifact Registry locations'
  local -r z_locations_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_ARTIFACTREGISTRY}${RBGC_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_V1}/projects/${RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID}/locations"
  rbgu_http_json "GET" "${z_locations_url}" "${z_token}" "region_validation"
  rbgu_http_require_ok "Validate region" "region_validation"

  local z_valid_regions
  z_valid_regions=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "region_validation" '.locations[].locationId' | tr '\n' ' ') || buc_die "Failed to parse region list"

  if ! [[ " ${z_valid_regions} " =~ [[:space:]]${z_region}[[:space:]] ]]; then
    buc_die "Invalid region. Valid regions: ${z_valid_regions}"
  fi

  buc_step 'Create depot project'
  local -r z_create_project_body="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_create_project.json"

  # OAuth users create projects without parent (per MPCR)
  jq -n \
    --arg projectId "${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}" \

params.intent: First-run inline fix for ₢BBAAX gauntlet observability gap: emit depot project ID and Cloud Build console URL via buc_info + buc_link immediately after rbgu_http_json_lro_ok succeeds creating the depot project in rbgp_depot_levy. Operator (and agent reading the live gauntlet output stream) needs the depot's Cloud Build console URL to peek at in-flight cloud builds during the long onboarding-sequence fixture; previously the URL was only emitted per-build via the existing buc_link calls in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1021 and :1257 (after each builds.create response), leaving long quiet stretches between builds where the URL was not visible in the recent stream. Reconstructing the project ID by hand from RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX + RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER yields the wrong identifier — RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID at rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:84 injects a 'd-' infix between the prefix and moniker (canc- + d- + canest2100004 = canc-d-canest2100004), and the agent missed this during BBAAX, sending the operator to a nonexistent project ID that produced the GCP 'You need additional access' error against canc-canest2100004 instead. Fix uses RBGC_CONSOLE_URL (already defined at rbgc_Constants.sh:78 as https://console.cloud.google.com/) suffixed with cloud-build/builds?project=${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}, matching the buc_link signature already established in rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh ('Click to ', 'Open depot Cloud Build console', URL). Did not promote ZRBFC_CLOUD_QUERY_BASE from rbfc to rbgc — the constant lives in foundry-private namespace appropriately, and rbgp's URL composition pattern at depot-levy time is short enough to inline. Preceded the buc_link with a buc_info echoing RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID itself so the project ID is grep-able from the output stream even if the link rendering is consumed by terminal styling. Insertion point is between the LRO completion and the billing-link step — the project exists in GCP at this point, all subsequent depot-levy steps continue against it, so the URL is meaningful from this moment forward. shellcheck -S error clean.

check progress.  four-mode is now redundant, right?  Now that we have the onboarding fixture?

content.text:
**Not redundant — partial overlap with distinct purposes.**

Comparison:

| Aspect | onboarding-sequence ordain_* (4 cases) | four-mode lifecycle (4 cases) |
|---|---|---|
| Ordain coverage | conjure / airgap / bind / graft | conjure / bind / graft / **kludge** |
| Vessel target | real production (rbev-sentry-deb-tether, rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter, etc.) | dedicated test vessels (`RBTDRC_FOURMODE_*_VESSEL_DIR`) |
| Post-ordain steps | `drive_hallmark` → push into consumer nameplates → git commit | `wrest` → docker run (busybox echo verify) → `summon` (image+about+vouch) → `plumb full` (SLSA/SBOM/Dockerfile markers) → `docker rmi` |
| What it actually tests | operator onboarding flow + downstream nameplate state for the gauntlet's crucible fixtures | full director-side ark pipeline: publication, retrieval, provenance display |

Two non-overlapping things four-mode owns:
1. **kludge mode** — onboarding-sequence uses kludge as setup (`kludge_tadmor`, `kludge_ccyolo`) but doesn't verify it as a mode lifecycle. four-mode does.
2. **wrest + summon + plumb verification per mode** — onboarding stops at hallmark-in-GAR; four-mode pulls back down and inspects provenance.

The real overlap: ordain-only step for conjure/bind/graft. If you want to dedupe, the move is to trim those three from four-mode (keep kludge + keep the wrest/summon/plumb verification on a single mode) — but that's a follow-up pace, not a BBAAX inline fix.

Standing by for next event.

content.text:
**Gauntlet failed at four-mode case 1 (`rbtdrc_fourmode_conjure_lifecycle`).** Suite-level fail-fast halted the remaining 4 fixtures (tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml) plus the other 3 four-mode cases.

**Fixtures green before the failure (98 cases total):**
- enrollment-validation 47, pristine-lifecycle 4, canonical-establish 4, onboarding-sequence 8, regime-validation 27, regime-smoke 8

**Failure mechanism:** Cloud Build for `rbev-busybox` conjure mode failed at **build step 4** — `USER_BUILD_STEP` type (the step's own command exited non-zero, not infrastructure). Steps 0-3 ran clean (alpine, docker, gcloud, docker). Step 4 (also docker) ran 38s then failed.

- Build ID: `c0dfb0d5-44c2-4dfb-9ce4-e35a3d100656`
- Vessel: `rbev-busybox`
- Hallmark: `c260503152320-r260503222324`
- Pool: `canc-canest2100004-pool-airgap` (conjure mode uses airgap pool)
- Reliquary: `r260503141803`

**Repair:** Open the build console and read step 4's log — the per-step stderr will say why the docker command exited non-zero. Cloud Build URL:

```
https://console.cloud.google.com/cloud-build/builds;region=us-central1/c0dfb0d5-44c2-4dfb-9ce4-e35a3d100656?project=canc-d-canest2100004
```

The pace stays open per the green-tally wrap criterion. Per the failure-mode contract, any retry will require fresh marshal-zero.

content.text:
Root cause found.

**Mechanism:**
- Build step 4 = buildx-push-image. Dockerfile reads `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}`, but `RBF_IMAGE_1` substitution is **empty** at build time — buildx surfaces the warning `InvalidDefaultArgInFrom: Default value for ARG ${RBF_IMAGE_1} results in empty or invalid base image name`.
- Empty `${RBF_IMAGE_1}` falls back to attempting `busybox:latest` resolution from Docker Hub.
- The build runs on the **airgap pool** — no internet egress. Docker Hub is unreachable: `dial tcp 44.220.103.105:443: i/o timeout`.

So two failures stacked:
1. **Substitution gap:** Foundry stitch did not inject the GAR-anchored base image reference into `RBF_IMAGE_1` for the four-mode conjure vessel.
2. **Airgap unreachability:** because of #1, buildx tried Docker Hub and the airgap pool correctly refused. (Airgap behavior is correct; the bug is upstream — the empty ARG should never have made it to a buildx call.)

Let me confirm which vessel directory the four-mode conjure case targets and check whether its rbrv.env has the anchor fields populated.

lastPrompt: gcloud is available to you for diagnostic purposes

280	  local -r z_vessel_dir="${2:-}"
281	  test -n "${z_token}"      || buc_die "zrbfd_registry_preflight: token required"
282	  test -n "${z_vessel_dir}" || buc_die "zrbfd_registry_preflight: vessel_dir required"
283	
284	  # --- Layer 1: Reliquary tool images ---
285	  zrbfd_preflight_reliquary "${z_token}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
286	
287	  # --- Layer 2: Enshrined base images ---
288	  # Each vessel declares base images via RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN (upstream tag) and
289	  # RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR (locator: package-path:tag). Enshrine copies the upstream
290	  # image into the enshrine namespace, pinned by content hash. The conjure
291	  # Dockerfile's FROM references the locator-resolved GAR ref, not upstream tag.
292	
293	  buc_step "Verifying enshrined base images exist in GAR"
294	
295	  local z_n=""
296	  local z_anchor_var=""
297	  local z_anchor=""
298	  local z_pkg_path=""
299	  local z_tag=""
300	  local z_origin_var=""
301	  local z_origin=""
302	  local z_any_checked="false"
303	  local z_status_file=""
304	  local z_response_file=""
305	  local z_stderr_file=""
306	  local z_http_code=""
307	
308	  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
309	    z_origin_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ORIGIN"
310	    z_anchor_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ANCHOR"
311	    z_origin="${!z_origin_var:-}"
312	    z_anchor="${!z_anchor_var:-}"
313	
314	    # Skip slots without an origin or without an anchor (pass-through images don't need enshrine)
315	    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue
316	    test -n "${z_anchor}" || continue
317	
318	    case "${z_anchor}" in
319	      *:*) : ;;
320	      *)   buc_die "Invalid ${z_anchor_var} locator format (expected package-path:tag): ${z_anchor}" ;;
321	    esac
322	    z_pkg_path="${z_anchor%:*}"
323	    z_tag="${z_anchor##*:}"
324	    test -n "${z_pkg_path}" || buc_die "Package path is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
325	    test -n "${z_tag}"      || buc_die "Tag is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
326	
327	    z_any_checked="true"
328	    z_status_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_status.txt"
329	    z_response_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_response.txt"
330	    z_stderr_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_stderr.txt"
331	
332	    curl --head -sS \
333	      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
334	      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
335	      -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}" \
336	      -H "Accept: ${ZRBFC_ACCEPT_MANIFEST_MTYPES}" \
337	      -w "%{http_code}" \
338	      -o "${z_response_file}" \
339	      "${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE}/${z_pkg_path}/manifests/${z_tag}" \
340	      > "${z_status_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
341	      || buc_die "HEAD request failed for enshrined image: ${z_anchor} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
342	
343	    z_http_code=$(<"${z_status_file}")
344	    test -n "${z_http_code}" || buc_die "HTTP status code is empty for enshrine check"
345

file.content:
local -r z_vessel_dir="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_token}"      || buc_die "zrbfd_registry_preflight: token required"
  test -n "${z_vessel_dir}" || buc_die "zrbfd_registry_preflight: vessel_dir required"

  # --- Layer 1: Reliquary tool images ---
  zrbfd_preflight_reliquary "${z_token}" "${z_vessel_dir}"

  # --- Layer 2: Enshrined base images ---
  # Each vessel declares base images via RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN (upstream tag) and
  # RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR (locator: package-path:tag). Enshrine copies the upstream
  # image into the enshrine namespace, pinned by content hash. The conjure
  # Dockerfile's FROM references the locator-resolved GAR ref, not upstream tag.

  buc_step "Verifying enshrined base images exist in GAR"

  local z_n=""
  local z_anchor_var=""
  local z_anchor=""
  local z_pkg_path=""
  local z_tag=""
  local z_origin_var=""
  local z_origin=""
  local z_any_checked="false"
  local z_status_file=""
  local z_response_file=""
  local z_stderr_file=""
  local z_http_code=""

  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
    z_origin_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ORIGIN"
    z_anchor_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ANCHOR"
    z_origin="${!z_origin_var:-}"
    z_anchor="${!z_anchor_var:-}"

    # Skip slots without an origin or without an anchor (pass-through images don't need enshrine)
    test -n "${z_origin}" || continue
    test -n "${z_anchor}" || continue

    case "${z_anchor}" in
      *:*) : ;;
      *)   buc_die "Invalid ${z_anchor_var} locator format (expected package-path:tag): ${z_anchor}" ;;
    esac
    z_pkg_path="${z_anchor%:*}"
    z_tag="${z_anchor##*:}"
    test -n "${z_pkg_path}" || buc_die "Package path is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"
    test -n "${z_tag}"      || buc_die "Tag is empty in ${z_anchor_var}: ${z_anchor}"

    z_any_checked="true"
    z_status_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_status.txt"
    z_response_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_response.txt"
    z_stderr_file="${ZRBFD_PREFLIGHT_PREFIX}enshrine_${z_n}_stderr.txt"

    curl --head -sS \
      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer ${z_token}" \
      -H "Accept: ${ZRBFC_ACCEPT_MANIFEST_MTYPES}" \
      -w "%{http_code}" \
      -o "${z_response_file}" \
      "${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_API_BASE}/${z_pkg_path}/manifests/${z_tag}" \
      > "${z_status_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
      || buc_die "HEAD request failed for enshrined image: ${z_anchor} — see ${z_stderr_file}"

    z_http_code=$(<"${z_status_file}")
    test -n "${z_http_code}" || buc_die "HTTP status code is empty for enshrine check"

    if test "${z_http_code}" = "404"; then
      buc_warn "Enshrined base image not found: ${z_anchor} (from ${z_origin})"
      buc_bare "  Enshrine copies upstream base images (e.g., busybox:latest from Docker Hub) into"
      buc_bare "  your private GAR, pinned by content hash. Like the reliquary, this ensures"
      buc_bare "  air-gapped builds never reach the public internet. The anchor locator is stable"
      buc_bare "  until you deliberately re-enshrine to pick up a newer upstream version."
      buc_bare "  Multiple vessels sharing the same base image anchor need only one enshrine."
      buc_bare "  Run enshrine, then re-run ordain:"
      buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
      buc_tabtarget "${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}" "${z_vessel_dir}"
      buc_die "Registry preflight failed — enshrined base image missing from GAR"
    elif test "${z_http_code}" != "200"; then
      buc_die "Unexpected HTTP ${z_http_code} when checking enshrined image: ${z_anchor}"
    fi

    buc_log_args "Enshrined image verified: ${z_anchor}"
  done

  if test "${z_any_checked}" = "true"; then
    buc_info "All enshrined base images verified in GAR"
  fi
}


zrbfd_stitch_build_json() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  local -r z_output_path="${1:?Output path required}"
  local -r z_hallmark="${2:?Hallmark required}"
  local -r z_context_tag="${3:?Context image tag required}"

  buc_log_args "Stitching builds.create JSON to ${z_output_path}"

  # Preconditions: vessel loaded and git state captured

content.text:
**Root cause confirmed — two stacked bugs.**

**Bug 1 (proximate): gauntlet sequencing gap.** `rbev-busybox` is the four-mode conjure vessel. Its `RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=busybox:latest` was blanked of its `RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR` by marshal-zero, and **nothing in the gauntlet re-enshrines it**. The onboarding-sequence inscribe_reliquary case mirrors *tool* images into GAR, but doesn't enshrine *vessel base* images. The other ordained vessels (sentry-deb-tether, anthropic-jupyter) survived with empty ANCHORs because they are `EGRESS_MODE=tether` — buildx fetched from Docker Hub directly. `rbev-busybox` is `EGRESS_MODE=airgap`, no internet egress, fetch times out at 38s with `dial tcp 44.220.103.105:443: i/o timeout`.

**Bug 2 (latent, exposed by Bug 1): preflight is permissive for airgap vessels with empty ANCHOR.** `Tools/rbk/rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:314-316` deliberately skips the enshrine-existence check when ANCHOR is empty (treats it as "pass-through image"). That semantic is correct for tether vessels but wrong for airgap vessels — there an empty ANCHOR is a guaranteed buildx-time failure that should die-fast at preflight with the existing 404-path diagnostic (line 346-356, which already prints the right "run enshrine then ordain" remediation).

Two repairs, distinct, both in scope of "first-run surfaced bugs":

- **Fix Bug 1** by ensuring `rbev-busybox` is enshrined before four-mode runs. Cleanest placement: add a setup step to the four-mode fixture (which currently has `setup: None`) — enshrine its airgap vessels' bases. Keeps four-mode self-contained; doesn't pollute onboarding-sequence with non-operator-facing test vessels.
- **Fix Bug 2** by tightening preflight at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:316`: when ANCHOR is empty AND `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=airgap` AND ORIGIN is non-empty, route into the existing diagnostic path. Defensive — catches future omissions before the 38s buildx timeout.

Per the failure-mode contract, any retry requires fresh marshal-zero. Per the green-tally wrap criterion, the pace stays open.

**Your call on segmentation:** inline both fixes here under ₢BBAAX, slate one or both as follow-up paces, or address only Bug 1 (proximate) and let Bug 2 wait. I'll wait for your decision before touching code.

message.content: Earlier in this chat I think we discussed eliminating four-mode entirely.  ARe there steps in four-mode that we should keep, but perhaps could perform during the onboarding fixture?

content.text:
Three things four-mode owns that onboarding-sequence doesn't:

1. **Standalone docker run of built image** — quick sanity that the image actually works (busybox echo).
2. **`summon`** — retriever pulls image+about+vouch arks back from GAR, verifies all three are locally inspectable.
3. **`plumb full`** — provenance display contains SLSA/SBOM/Dockerfile markers.

(The fourth four-mode case, `kludge_lifecycle`, is already covered by `kludge_tadmor`+`kludge_ccyolo` followed by the tadmor crucible fixture consuming them.)

These three are all retriever-facing operations that run on the operator machine — fast, no cloud cost. Cleanest absorption: append `wrest → docker run → summon → plumb` as a tail on each existing onboarding ordain case (`ordain_conjure`, `ordain_airgap`, `ordain_bind`, `ordain_graft`). That converts "did it build" coverage into "did the full director→retriever roundtrip work" coverage, kills four-mode entirely, and removes the rbev-busybox/rbev-graft-demo/rbev-bottle-plantuml four-mode test vessels from the maintenance surface.

Tradeoff: onboarding-sequence cases get longer (~4 extra ops per case, all local); the cases now do something the docket frames as build-only. The `## Build/run split — load-bearing` paddock note specifically reserves run-side coverage for crucible fixtures, but standalone docker-run-the-image-with-echo is verification the *build* worked, not run-side functionality, so I read this as fitting the build side.

Want me to draft this as a follow-up pace under ₣BB (kill four-mode + absorb into onboarding), then resume BBAAX with the gauntlet re-run as the verifier?

lastPrompt: Earlier in this chat I think we discussed eliminating four-mode entirely.  ARe there steps in four-mode that we should keep, but perhaps could perform during the onboarding fixture?

message.content: I think I like the approach to kill four-mode and retain some of its steps.  Do you advise adjusting the Onboarding handbooks?  I think we may not need to, if this is just 'post onboarding checking'.  ?

content.text:
Right — handbooks shouldn't change. The build half of each ordain case continues walking the handbook track (regression coverage preserved); the new tail is **gauntlet-internal verification** — "after Cloud Build returned SUCCESS, prove the artifact is actually retrievable + functional + has provenance." That's a property a regression suite checks, not a procedure an operator follows.

One small caveat: `rbw-Oda` (airgap) handbook already includes "compare plumb" per the tabtarget context, so its case will exercise plumb twice (once via handbook walk, once via the new gauntlet tail). Harmless duplication; could be noted in the pace but doesn't need fixing.

The framing earns its keep in the docket: **handbook = operator's recipe; gauntlet = regression that runs the recipe AND adds artifact-verification.** Cleanly explains why the build half is handbook-driven and the verification tail isn't.

message.content: I'm incliined to slate this as the first pace in this heat, solve it, then repeat the current pace.  Any counterpoint to this plan?

content.text:
Two counterpoints worth a moment before you commit:

**1. Bug 2 goes dormant.** The preflight-permissive bug at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:316` (empty ANCHOR + airgap = silent fall-through to 38s buildx timeout) was only surfaced by four-mode. Kill four-mode and the bug becomes invisible-but-still-present — any future airgap conjure vessel anyone adds will hit it. Either fold a one-line preflight tightening into the kill-four-mode pace (cheap), slate it as an itch, or accept the latency.

**2. Vessel deletion blast radius.** Killing four-mode logically lets you delete its three test vessels (`rbev-busybox`, `rbev-bottle-plantuml`, `rbev-graft-demo`). But `rbev-bottle-plantuml` is the bind-mode vessel for the **pluml crucible fixture** — can't delete. `rbev-graft-demo` may have other refs too. Worth a `grep -r` sanity check before the kill commit lands, or just don't delete vessels in this pace and let a follow-up cleanup decide.

Otherwise the plan is sound — the pace sequence reads cleanly (kill-four-mode → BBAAX-rerun where BBAAX verifies the kill), and the framing of "handbook = recipe, gauntlet = regression + verification" is articulable in the docket.
